questions.scenario: A publish stage needs a registry username and password. The current Jenkinsfile binds them at pipeline level, so compile, test, and publish all receive the values. Which change creates the narrowest useful runtime boundary?

options.text: Bind the credential at pipeline level but clear the variables before compile and test stages begin.
Bind the credential at the publish-stage level so every command in that stage receives it.
Use a file credential in a workspace subdirectory and remove it after the registry push.
Bind the credential inside the gated publish stage and only around login and push operations.

questions.explanation: withCredentials should wrap the smallest block that needs the secret. Global bindings expose it to more commands, workspace files can persist, and command-line arguments may appear in process listings. See [Jenkins credentials binding](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/pipeline/steps/credentials-binding/).

questions.scenario: During a red-team exercise, Jenkins masks a deployment token when a tool prints it directly, but a pipeline author base64-encodes the same token and sends it to an external endpoint. The security team must prevent deliberate exfiltration, not only accidental log disclosure. Which conclusion should guide the remediation?

options.text: Masking is sufficient when the job runs on an isolated agent with no production network route.
Masking reduces accidental logs; author trust, credential scope, and isolation stop deliberate misuse.
The encoded value will remain hidden if the original token is registered with the masking engine.
The credential remains protected if only approved shared-library code performs the encoding.

questions.explanation: Masking is a log hygiene feature, not a sandbox for a credential-aware author. A job that receives a secret can transform or transmit it. The stronger controls decide who can edit that code and which job can receive the value. See [Jenkins Credentials Binding plugin](https://plugins.jenkins.io/credentials-binding/).

questions.scenario: A shell step places API_TOKEN inside a Groovy double-quoted string before invoking curl. Reviewers want the shell, not Groovy, to expand the secret within the credential block. Which form is safer?

options.text: Use a single-quoted Groovy string so the shell expands the bound environment variable.
Use Groovy interpolation so the secret becomes part of the generated command before the shell starts.
Write the fully expanded command to a protected artifact and delete it after the deployment audit.
Use Groovy interpolation but disable shell tracing before the command runs.

questions.explanation: Keeping expansion in the shell reduces the chance that the value appears in Groovy-interpolated step metadata or process arguments. It does not replace masking and scope, but it avoids an unnecessary exposure path. See [Jenkins credentials binding step guidance](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/pipeline/steps/credentials-binding/).

questions.scenario: A sandboxed Jenkinsfile requests approval for a Java method that can enumerate controller items without respecting the caller's permissions. What should the administrator do first?

options.text: Approve it for this folder because only administrators can edit jobs there.
Approve the signature temporarily, then remove the approval after the release window.
Review permission impact, prefer a supported step or narrow trusted wrapper, and deny broad access.
Move the operation into a globally trusted library maintained by the same service team.

questions.explanation: Script approval is global, not tied to one job, so each signature needs a security review. A normal Pipeline step or tightly owned wrapper is safer than granting arbitrary controller access. Folder libraries remain sandboxed. See [Jenkins in-process Script Approval](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/script-approval/).

questions.scenario: A public fork pull request changes a test helper, not the Jenkinsfile. The PR job wraps the test command in withCredentials for a production token. Why is the token still at risk?

options.text: The pull request can alter transitive test dependencies that execute before the credential block.
The test helper remains trusted because only the Jenkinsfile, not application code, can access bound environment variables.
The long-lived agent may retain the token in process or workspace state after the test completes.
User-controlled test code runs inside the credential scope and can read or transmit the token.

questions.explanation: An attacker does not need to edit the Jenkinsfile if code they control executes while the credential is present. Untrusted tests should run without production credentials, and deployment bindings should sit behind trusted branch or job boundaries. See [Jenkins multibranch credential security](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/security/securing-org-folders-and-multibranch-pipelines/).

questions.scenario: Jenkins issues an OIDC token whose audience is sts.amazonaws.com and subject names the checkout-api main job. AWS accepts the identity but the deploy receives AccessDenied on an unrelated S3 bucket. Which interpretation is correct?

options.text: OIDC succeeded, but the token audience should be changed from STS to S3 for bucket access.
Federation worked; the role or bucket policy intentionally denies that unrelated bucket.
The role assumption succeeded, but the session duration expired before the S3 request.
The role trust policy accepted too narrow a subject and must be broadened to include the bucket.

questions.explanation: Authentication and authorization are separate. Accepted OIDC claims prove the role assumption path worked; the role or resource policy then limits what the session may do. Least privilege should make unrelated access fail. See [AWS IAM OIDC federation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_providers_oidc.html).

questions.scenario: A Jenkins controller builds public and private repositories and can deploy to production AWS accounts. Which controls work together to protect its deployment credentials?

options.text: Store secrets in a credential provider and bind them only around the required steps.
Run credential-aware steps only on isolated agents but allow untrusted branches to invoke those steps.
Keep untrusted branches and user-controlled tests outside production credential scopes.
Use globally trusted libraries for deployment logic while allowing each service team to merge library changes.
Prefer short-lived federated roles with restricted claims and permissions where the platform supports them.

questions.explanation: Storage and runtime scope reduce exposure, branch or author controls decide who can execute secret-aware code, and federation shortens credential lifetime. Masking and rushed approvals cannot replace those trust boundaries. See [Jenkins credentials](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/security/credentials/) and [Script Approval](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/script-approval/).
